focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 16

The platform had started to smell wrong before the train stopped.

Citrus oil from the hospitality diffusers still leaked out of the polished demo coach in thin sweet breaths, but the heat had already turned it sour. Brake dust sat in the roof trusses. Pigeon droppings baked on the girders over Platform B. Under that lived the tank smell Marisol knew better than prayer: warm rinse water, soap gone rotten, old piss lifted back into the air by pressure and metal. RailPassage called the side line a greywater purge because investors liked categories that sounded domestic. Anybody who had ever cleaned the trap screens knew what rode in it when the line was run hard and the trauma-room sink was tied in with the lavatory wash.

Marisol stood on the service side of Car Seven with her supervisor passcard damp in her palm and counted tiles, banner poles, camera tripods, the yellow tactile strip, the sandwich kiosk with its rolling shutter half open, the gap where a municipal drain should have been and was not. Four feet south and the purge would hit the maintenance trench and disappear under the platform lip. Four feet north and it would sheet across the public walking lane, right where the local cameras had set up beside the welcome flags that said HUMANE MOBILITY, SHARED FUTURES in three languages and one font too expensive for a corridor that still sent workers home with bleach burns.

She checked the car marks again. Teresa had wanted the polished demonstration coach centered under the glass canopy, where the commuters, ministers, and hired journalists could look into bunks fitted with clean blankets and sealed fruit cups. Ivo was making sure the wrong car met the crowd.

He crouched in the coupling gap one car down, broad back bent, blue glitter still hanging f...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...rch and false welcome. "Once he's on the consist."

Ivo rapped his knuckles against the polished demonstration lavatory. The plastic greenery shook. "Car Three still has a dry service chase behind this pretty toilet. We pulled the inspection panel for the retrofit and never put the security screws back. He can wedge between the wall skin and the purge pipe. Hot, ugly, no room to stretch, but the vent fan gives him air. He stays there till the public-side doors open."

Marisol looked at the white bowl, at the fake plant, at the whole polished farce standing there on borrowed bolts. "If Gabriel gets out," she said, "he gets out where people can see what he was almost fed into. Not by being the one clean bag Teresa carries upstairs."

Ivo studied her a moment longer. Then he reached for the county notice, folded it once, and stuffed it deeper into his pocket as if to stop himself from touching it again.

"All right," he said. "If we're ruining ourselves, we might as well do it in good light."

Marisol nodded. The service fan kept rattling. Above them, through steel and tile, somebody on the platform tested the welcome script again in two languages, all clipped politeness and synthetic calm. She could almost see Teresa upstairs waiting for names, confident that motherhood would return to her office carrying obedience in both hands.

Instead Marisol tore off the clean top sheet and wrote four things on it in block capitals, the way she wrote shutdown orders when there could be no ambiguity. If tomorrow had to start inside Teresa's hospitality show, it would start on their terms: `PROPERTY CARTS ABOVE GRADE.` `PURGE LINE LIVE.` `DOORS OPEN IN PUBLIC.` `RETIRO LOAD ON MANUAL SCAN.`

Then she slid the paper to him and said, "Let's make them own what they've been calling clean."
